exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 1: Complete the second sentence using the given word (key word transformation)

questions.grammarRule: 'Have a good time' is a fixed idiom meaning 'enjoy yourself'. After the auxiliary 'did', the main verb stays in its base form: 'Did you have...?' — not 'had', which would incorrectly repeat the past tense.
'What do you do for a living?' is the standard idiom for asking about someone's job. Since 'What do' already starts the question, the blank continues with subject + verb + article: 'you do for a'.

'Whose class were you in?' asks about possession using 'whose' + noun. Since the subject is 'you', the verb must agree: 'were', not 'was'. ('Who's' is a contraction of 'who is/has' — different word entirely.)

'Have a meal' is an idiom meaning 'to eat'. Since the original sentence is in the past ('We ate'), the transformation needs the past form: 'had a meal'. Also, 'meal' (not 'food') is the correct noun in this idiom.
'Good at' + gerund (-ing form) describes being skilled at something. English uses the adjective 'good' here, not the adverb 'well' — 'good at singing', not 'well at singing'.
'Learn how to' + base infinitive describes acquiring a skill. After 'to', use the plain verb form: 'how to drive', not 'how to driving'.

promptParts.after: for our holidays when I was young.

questions.grammarRule: 'Used to' + base infinitive describes a habitual action in the past that no longer happens. It's spelled 'used to' (not 'use to'), and it's followed by the plain verb form: 'used to go', not 'used to going' or 'used to went'.
'Fly' is an irregular verb: past tense is 'flew' (not 'flied'). Since the original sentence is in simple past ('was'), the transformation keeps simple past: 'Our plane flew over...'.
'Every time' + present simple describes a habitual action. Since the subject is 'she' (third person singular), the verb needs the -s ending: 'goes', not 'go' or 'going'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 2: Rewrite the second sentence using the given word

questions.grammarRule: 'All the time' is a fixed phrase meaning 'always/continuously' and needs the article 'the' between 'all' and 'time' — 'all the time', not 'all time'.
'In order to' + base infinitive expresses purpose (why someone does something). It needs 'to' before the base verb: 'in order to apologize', not 'in order apologize' or '...apologizing'.

promptParts.after: bring skis as they are included in the package.

questions.grammarRule: 'Don't have to' means something is not necessary (you have a choice). Careful: 'mustn't' has a different meaning — it means something is forbidden, not optional.
'Under the impression that...' is a fixed idiom meaning 'I believed/thought that...'. It's always singular ('the impression', not 'the impressions') and always needs the article 'the'.

promptParts.before: She continued to cry until he

questions.grammarRule: 'Out of sight' is a fixed idiom (no article needed) meaning 'no longer visible'. It pairs with 'be' to describe a state: 'he was out of sight', not 'went out of sight' or 'out of the sight'.
'Beyond my means' is a fixed idiom meaning 'too expensive for me to afford'. 'Means' here (financial resources) is always plural, even though it can look like a singular word.

'Have/has + past participle + since + [starting point]' (Present Perfect) describes an action that started in the past and continues now. Since the subject is 'they', use 'have': 'they have lived...', not 'has lived' or 'had lived'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 3: Put the verb in capitals into the past tense

promptParts.before: Nicholas Evans was a child genius. He was born in 1980 and his mother
promptParts.after: him how to read when he was eight months old.

questions.grammarRule: 'Teach' is an irregular verb: past tense is 'taught' (not 'teached', a common regularization error). This passage uses simple past throughout to narrate completed past events.

promptParts.after: his first book when he was only one.

questions.grammarRule: 'Read' is unusual: its past tense is spelled the same as the present ('read') but pronounced differently (like 'red'). There's no '-ed' added — 'readed' doesn't exist.

promptParts.after: him a piano for his third birthday.

questions.grammarRule: 'Buy' is an irregular verb: past tense is 'bought' (not 'buyed').

promptParts.before: He got a piano for his third birthday and he

questions.grammarRule: 'Begin' is irregular: simple past is 'began'. Don't confuse it with 'begun', which is the past participle used with 'have/has' (e.g. 'has begun'), not for simple past narration.

promptParts.after: his first piece of music soon after getting a piano.

questions.grammarRule: 'Write' is irregular: simple past is 'wrote'. Don't confuse it with 'written', the past participle used with 'have/has' (e.g. 'has written'), not for simple past narration.

promptParts.before: By the age of seven he

questions.grammarRule: 'Speak' is irregular: simple past is 'spoke'. 'Spoken' is the past participle (used with have/has), not the simple past form.

promptParts.before: By the age of seven he spoke three languages fluently and he

questions.grammarRule: 'Can' is a modal verb; its past tense form is 'could' (not 'canned', which isn't a real inflection of this modal).
'Find' is irregular: past tense is 'found'. Don't confuse it with 'founded', which is the past tense of the different verb 'found' (meaning 'to establish', like founding a company).

promptParts.after: his arm the first time he played football.

questions.grammarRule: 'Break' is irregular: simple past is 'broke'. 'Broken' is the past participle (used with have/has), not the simple past form.

promptParts.after: any races he took part in.

questions.grammarRule: 'Lose' is irregular: past tense is 'lost'. Careful not to confuse the spelling with 'loose' (meaning 'not tight') — that's a different word entirely.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 5: Open cloze — think of one word which best fits each space

promptParts.before: In many ways young people between the ages of twelve and nineteen are not very different now from how they

questions.grammarRule: 'Used to' + infinitive describes a repeated past habit that's no longer true. The word needed is 'used' (with -d), not 'use' — a very common spelling slip.

promptParts.before: Every year the number of cigarettes they smoke is

questions.grammarRule: After the auxiliary 'is' (present continuous), the main verb needs the -ing form: 'is going down', not 'is go/goes/went down'.

promptParts.after: eat so much fast food or drink so much alcohol.

questions.grammarRule: The passage describes current habits in present simple, and the subject is 'they' (plural), so the negative auxiliary is 'don't' — not 'doesn't' (for he/she/it) or 'didn't' (past tense).

promptParts.before: However, the number of televisions and computers in teenagers' bedrooms is
promptParts.after: , something that a lot of parents worry about.

questions.grammarRule: After 'is' (present continuous), the verb needs the -ing form: 'is increasing', describing an ongoing trend.
The context (parents worrying about their teenage children's screen time) points to 'parents' as the fitting noun — plural, since 'a lot of' + a countable noun needs the plural form.

promptParts.after: a lot of time in their rooms playing computer games

questions.grammarRule: 'They' is plural, so the present-tense verb has no -s: 'they spend', not 'spends'. The context describes a present habitual fact, not the past ('spent').

promptParts.before: And she thinks that this is a complete

questions.grammarRule: 'A waste of time' is a fixed idiomatic phrase meaning something isn't worth the time spent on it — it doesn't work with other nouns like 'a loss of time'.

promptParts.before: They always tell me that they're doing their homework, but I know they

questions.grammarRule: This is a short reply avoiding repetition: 'I know they aren't [doing their homework]' mirrors the auxiliary 'are' from 'they're doing'. Since the subject is 'they', the negative form is 'aren't', not 'isn't' or 'don't'.

promptParts.before: When I was their age, I really enjoyed

questions.grammarRule: 'Enjoy' is followed by the -ing form (gerund), not the infinitive: 'enjoyed being outside', not 'enjoyed to be/be/been outside'.

promptParts.before: But Steve and Robert only seem
promptParts.after: in the latest CDs or what's on television that night.

questions.grammarRule: 'Interested' (feeling interest) is different from 'interesting' (causing interest). Since Steve and Robert are the ones who feel curious about something, 'interested' is correct: 'seem interested in...'.
